title: [BUG] SkiaSharp rendering performance is much lower than native Skia C++
body:
### Description

There is a large performance gap between SkiaSharp and equivalent native Skia C++ code. The C++ version achieves around 120 fps while the managed SkiaSharp code achieves less than 10 fps by default, and up to 40 fps with new OpenGL flags. Benchmarks and sample code are available below.

Benchmark repo: https://github.com/wieslawsoltes/FastSkiaSharp
Fork with downgraded Skia (same version as SkiaSharp): https://github.com/mattleibow/FastSkiaSharp/tree/main_119
Discussion thread: https://x.com/wieslawsoltes/status/1984761177520685154
Investigation PR: https://github.com/mono/SkiaSharp/pull/3418

### Code

Repository examples and minimal repros:
- https://github.com/wieslawsoltes/FastSkiaSharp
- https://github.com/mattleibow/FastSkiaSharp/tree/main_119

No standalone code snippet, see full repo above.

### Expected Behavior

SkiaSharp rendering performance should be much closer to that of native Skia for equivalent rendering workloads, ideally within 80–90% of the native C++ performance.

### Actual Behavior

SkiaSharp rendering is much slower than native Skia, showing frame rates <10 fps in default configuration and around 40 fps with new OpenGL flags, compared to 120 fps for native C++.

### Version of SkiaSharp

3.116.0 (Current)

### Last Known Good Version of SkiaSharp

Other (Please indicate in the description)

### IDE / Editor

Visual Studio Code (macOS)

### Platform / Operating System

macOS

### Platform / Operating System Version

_No response_

comments.author: mattleibow
comments.body:
## Investigation Summary

**Root cause found and fixed in #3546.**

`SKGLView` on macOS reads stencil bits via `glGetIntegerv(GL_STENCIL_BITS)` which returns **0** on macOS default framebuffers — a known driver quirk. Native Skia reads from `NSOpenGLPixelFormat` instead (returns the correct value: 8). Without stencil, Skia can't use its fast `TessellationPathRenderer` and falls back to the much slower `DefaultPathRenderer`, which CPU-tessellates every path individually.

**One-line fix → 15.6× speedup:**

| Config | Before | After | Native C++ |
|---|---|---|---|
| GL (40k paths) | **5.3 fps** | **77–93 fps** | 61 fps |
| Metal (40k paths) | 15.5 fps | 15.5 fps | 15.5 fps |

### Interesting findings along the way

- **Metal had zero overhead** — SKMetalView matched native C++ Metal frame-for-frame at all complexity levels. The reported gap is purely GL.
- **Native C++ GL without MSAA is also slow** — only 4.6 fps at 40k paths. The native app's speed comes entirely from 4× MSAA enabling `TessellationPathRenderer`, not from being "native."
- **The reported "120 fps" was VSync-masked** — disabling VSync revealed native GL actually runs at ~61 fps. Still fast, but not 120.
- **macOS `glGetIntegerv(GL_STENCIL_BITS)` lies** — returns 0 for the default framebuffer even when 8 stencil bits are allocated. This is a macOS-specific GL driver quirk that the native Skia codebase works around in `GLWindowContext_mac.mm:127`.
- **After the fix, C# GL is faster than native C++ GL** — 77–93 fps vs 61 fps. The .NET overhead is negligible; the bottleneck was entirely in Skia's path renderer selection.

Full investigation details (12 benchmark apps, per-phase profiling, isolation experiments, AI model corroboration) are on the PR: #3546.
